label: Chapter 1
title: History of Hypnosis & Signs of Hypnosis
content:
CHAPTER 2 
HISTORY OF HYPNOSIS 
Shamanism in Ancient Egypt  
Dark Room 
EGYPTIAN SLEEP TEMPLE 
THE GREEK TEMPLE OF ASKLEPIOS 
THE EGYPTIAN TEMPLE OF IMHOTEP 
ANCIENT CHRISTIANITY OF HYPNOSIS 
the modern fundamental Christian perspective 
Hypnosis is a tool for expanding awareness  
beyond the five senses 
see [visual: V],  
hear [auditory: A],  
feeling [kinesthetic: K],  
smell [olfactory: O],  
taste [gustatory: G] 
Anton Mesmer 
“Hypnosis entered the 
modern medical world 
 in the 1772 when a  
German physician,  
Anton Mesmer (1734-1815), 
introduced his theory  
of animal magnetism.” 
“One of Mesmer's disciples, Marquis de Puysegur, believed that the cosmic fluid 
described by Mesmer was not magnetic, but electric.” 
John Elliotson 
“In the 1840's a British surgeon, John Elliotson (1791-1868), performed many 
operations painlessly using animal magnetism.” 
Dr. Esdaile

Another British physician, Dr. Esdaile (1808-1859), observed a demonstration of 
animal magnetism while on vacation in England. He returned to India and performed 
over 
3,000 operations in a prison hospital using nothing but animal magnetism. 
James Braid 
“In 1840, the word "hypnosis" was coined by James Braid (1795-1860). Braid 
stumbled upon the state when, while treating a patient with an eye disorder, another 
man 
requiring immediate attention arrived at Braid’s office.” 
“Braid decided that the state was a nervous sleep.” 
“Braid‘s research in hypnosis was published in many languages before he realized 
that the state is not sleep. When he tried to rename the phenomena "mono-ideaism" in 
order to more accurately describe the enhanced awareness and focal capacity of the 
hypnosis experience, but "hypnosis" was already accepted as the common term, and 
has 
remained so until the present.” 
Dr. Jean Martin Charcot 
“In the late 1870s, Dr. Jean Martin Charcot (1825-1893), a neurologist and head of the 
School of Salpetriere in France, described three states of hypnosis:” 
1 - lethargy, (physical relaxation), 
2 - catalepsy, (limbs remaining in any position they are placed),  
3 - somnambulism (able to walk, talk and be anesthetized). 
“During the 1880s, two French physicians, Hippolyte Bernheim (1837-1919) and 
Auguste Ambroise Liebeault (1823-1904), established a joint practice adopting 
Braid's ideas 
of induction via suggestion.” 
“Their work was so successful that physicians 
from around the world came to study, including Sigmund Freud. Freud was 
researching the use of hypnosis to unlock repressed memories, and was not a good 
student of hypnosis because of his mistaken belief that deep trance was needed for

hypnotic success. Freud, as with many modern hypnotists, was only able to obtain 
deep trance with a small percentage of his clients.” 
“His colleague, Joseph Breuer (1842 - 1925), was the leading medical hypnotist of the 
day. Breuer discovered that symptoms were reduced or disappeared after the subject 
was able to describe their experience. He developed the concept of using catharsis as a 
tool for healing, with hypnosis to accelerate the process” 
After discussing Breuer's findings with him, Freud put aside hypnosis and developed 
his theories of psychoanalysis. He took a position that humans have both conscious 
and unconscious processes. Using an iceberg or island as the model, the observed 
portion above the water represents conscious processes, the submerged portion 
represents unconscious processes. Carl Jung (1875-1961) added a collective 
unconcious to Freud’s concepts of depth psychology and the structure of personality. 
Emile Couie 
“Emile Couie (1857-1926), a French lay psychotherapist, founded a therapeutic 
method based on the auto-suggestion(self induced suggestion), "Day by day and in 
every 
way I am getting better and better." 
Phineas Quimby 
Phineas Quimby, a European mesmerist, toured the United States working with 
people suffering from all types of illnesses. One woman he helped, Mary Morse (nee 
Baker) Eddie (1821-1910), studied with him and went on to found the Christian 
Science Church. Mary Baker Eddie refused to use the word hypnosis or mention it as 
part of the foundation for her teachings, she renamed this familiar process the Science 
of the Mind. Her teachings incorporated awareness of the positive and negative effect 
of belief on themind. 
Edgar Cayce 
In 1901, a man named Edgar Cayce discovered he could diagnose illnesses and 
treatments while in trance. Edgar Cayce, the Sleeping Prophet, would give detailed 
diagnosis of clients’ problems without ever meeting some of them in person. Cayce’s 
capacities point to the transpersonal or non-local aspects of mind and being. Cayce 
died in 1945, but his large library of material which he obtained remotely during 
trance are available for research at the Association for Research and 
Enlightenment(A.R.E.) in Virginia Beach.

Milton Erickson 
Milton Erickson (1901-1980) is known as the founder of modern medical hypnosis. 
He contracted polio as a kid and had to relearn how to walk, which he did by watching 
his 
younger sibling (sister?). His style is a storytelling, non-directive style of hypnotic 
interventions for improved health. 
Dave Elman 
In 1908, an eight year old boy named Dave Elman became interested in hypnosis. His 
father was dying of cancer, and a stage hypnotist helped Dave’s father live pain free 
forthe last weeks of his life. Elman was inspired. He read everything he could find 
abouthypnosis. Everything he read indicated that a successful hypnotic induction 
might takeanywhere from several minutes to several hours, and required a bright light 
or other fixation device. While practicing, Elman noticed boredom and eye fatigue. 
He learned from an eye doctor how to obtain eye fatigue more rapidly, and developed 
methods of hypnotic 
induction that reduced the induction time tremendously. Methods such as the Elman 
Two Finger Technique made hypnosis a more viable tool for medicaluse (see Elman, 
Hypnotherapy, Chapter 7, pp.41-50). Elman concluded that the subject was always in 
complete control, able to suspend disbelief from what he called the critical faculty or 
critical factor of the mind, and able to receive pleasing suggestions. He noted that 
when improper suggestions were was given, one of two things happened: the subject 
would reject the suggestion, and/or terminate trance. 
“There were only a couple hundred people in the United State using hypnosis in 1945. 
By 1955, the British Medical Association reported that hypnosis was a valuable tool 
in medical treatment. It urged that hypnosis be taught in medical schools, and finally 
gave a nod of approval for physicians to openly learn and practice hypnosis. In 1958, 
hypnosis was given approval as a medical modality by the American Medical 
Association.” 
“In 1975, the title Hypnotherapist (079.157.010) was listed in the Dictionary of 
Occupational Titles published by the U.S. Department of Labor. There are thousands 
of trained hypnotists worldwide. Nonetheless, there are organizations actively seeking 
to limit the free and open practice of hypnosis.”

By 1955, the British Medical Association reported that hypnosis was a valuable tool 
in medical treatment. It urged that hypnosis be taught in medical schools, and finally 
gave a nod of approval for physicians to openly learn and practice hypnosis. In 1958,

hypnosis was given approval as a medical modality by the American Medical 
Association.” 
“In 1975, the title Hypnotherapist (079.157.010) was listed in the Dictionary of 
Occupational Titles published by the U.S. Department of Labor. There are thousands 
of trained hypnotists worldwide. Nonetheless, there are organizations actively seeking 
to limit the free and open practice of hypnosis.”

label: Chapter 2
title: Nature of Suggestions
content:
CHAPTER 4 
 
NATURE OF SUGGESTIONS 
Suggestible vs. Gullible  
  
Suggestible and gullible are related but distinct concepts:  
1. Suggestible refers to a person's ability to be influenced by suggestions, ideas, or 
external  
cues. It means someone is open to new information or perspectives and may be more 
likely  
to change their behavior or beliefs based on what they are exposed to. Being 
suggestible is  
a normal psychological trait and can be a helpful tool in areas like learning, creativity, 
and  
therapy. Suggestibility does not imply a lack of critical thinking, but rather an 
openness to  
influence.  
2. Gullible, on the other hand, refers to a person who is easily deceived or tricked, 
often due  
to a lack of discernment or critical thinking. A gullible individual tends to believe 
things too  
readily, even when the information may be misleading, false, or irrational. While  
suggestibility involves openness to influence in a constructive way, gullibility 
suggests a  
vulnerability to manipulation or exploitation.  
In summary:  
• Suggestible people are open to new ideas and influences in a balanced way.  
Responding to Suggestion  
• Gullible people are overly trusting and can be easily deceived, often without 
questioning  
the validity of what they're told. 
Responding to Suggestion:  
People respond to suggestion in varying ways, and some individuals are more 
responsive than  
others. The level of response to suggestion is determined by the individual and can

change  
depending on factors like emotional state and environment. At any given time, 
individuals have  
the ability to accept or reject a suggestion, and this decision can happen either 
instantly or  
gradually.  
Instantly Accepting Suggestions:  
Some people respond to suggestions immediately, especially  
when there is a strong desire or emotional state influencing them. For example, 
entering a hospital  
or medical setting can trigger a trance-like state in some individuals, leading to an 
immediate  
response to suggestions. In these situations, formal hypnosis is not always necessary 
for the  
suggestions to be effective. The environment and state of being play a key role in how 
a person  
responds.  
Gradually Accepting Suggestions:  
Suggestions can also take hold gradually. This can happen  
through repeated exposure or compounding suggestions, where each suggestion builds 
upon the  
previous one. A common technique is to start with simple, easy-to-accept suggestions 
and slowly  
build on them. Repetitive, open-ended suggestions like "Every day in every way, I am 
getting  
better and better" can also lead to gradual change.  
Taking Responsibility:  
Individuals are ultimately responsible for how they think and respond to  
suggestions. They have the power to accept or reject any suggestion, and no one can 
be made to  
do anything against their core beliefs, values, or desires solely through suggestion. 
The level of  
response to suggestion is often linked to the individual’s level of desire and 
willingness to change.  
Inner Lie Detector:

“I am a woman.” (And notice how you feel in your body.) 
"I am a man." (And notice how you feel in your body.) 
“I am breathing in air." (Notice how you feel in the body.) 
"I am breathing in charcoal dust." (Notice how you feel in the body.) 
Beliefs and Selective thinking:  
Beliefs play a major role in how suggestions are accepted. These  
beliefs are often shaped by upbringing, culture, and experiences. People tend to filter 
incoming  
suggestions based on whether they align with their existing beliefs, and they are more 
likely to  
accept suggestions that fit their view of themselves and the world. 
How to Deliver Suggestions:  
When delivering suggestions, confidence is key. The hypnotist must  
convey complete certainty that the suggestions will be accepted and acted upon. 
Doubt or  
uncertainty can undermine the effectiveness of the suggestion. The delivery should be 
calm,  
confident, and positive, with an emphasis on present or future positive outcomes. 
Hypnotists  
should also match the subject’s breathing and tone to establish rapport and deepen the  
suggestibility.  
Pre-Hypnotic Suggestion (Pretalk):  
The pretalk is essential to set expectations and clarify the goals  
of the session. It prepares the subject by explaining what to expect, dislodging any 
misconceptions  
about hypnosis, and creating a positive mental environment for the session. Educating 
the subject  
about hypnosis increases the likelihood of a successful experience.  
Being in the Moment:  
During the session, the hypnotist should speak in the present tense,  
affirming what is happening as it occurs. This helps deepen the rapport and keeps the 
subject  
engaged in the process. By acknowledging the subject’s progress and encouraging 
them to

continue, the hypnotist reinforces a sense of well-being and relaxation, helping to 
deepen the  
state of hypnosis and maximize the effectiveness of the suggestions. In summary, 
people respond to suggestion differently, and the ability to accept or reject  
suggestions is influenced by desire, beliefs, environment, and state of being. Effective 
suggestion  
delivery requires confidence, positive reinforcement, and a deep understanding of the 
subject's  
needs and state of mind. 
Post-Hypnotic Review 
Once the experience is complete, discuss what happened. 
“Now tell me about that.” 
“Kind of surprised yourself? Tell me about that.” 
“Is there anything that sticks out from your experience that you would like to share 
with me? 
“Is there anything you found particularly interesting?” 
“Is there anything you observed or experienced that sticks out for you?” 
Compounding Suggestions 
"Go deeper into droopy, drowsy slumber as your eyelids get heavy and close and you 
go deeper into the knowing." 
“The use of and, as, and but correlates the ideas into one thought.” 
“The eyes generally blink with each new thought.” 
“As you breathe /pause/, the rising and falling of the rib cage /pause/ gives all the 
internal organs /pause/a beautiful massage /pause/and you know /pause/, you know 
how /pause/how good /pause/ a good massage feels." 
“You are breathing. Your rib cage is rising and falling with each breath. The 
movement of your rib cage is massaging the internal organs. You know. You know 
how. Massage feels good. Feel. Breathing feels good.Breathing massages the internal 
organs,” etc. “Anything I do will increase your _______ (relaxation, relationship, 
numbness, forgetfulness, studying, test taking, awareness, sporting performance, 
anesthesia . . . etc.).” 
Post-Hypnotic Suggestion  
A post-hypnotic suggestion is any suggestion made during a hypnotic trance that the 
individual will  
carry out after the trance has ended. This type of suggestion can take effect 
immediately, after

some time, or sometimes not at all. The response to post-hypnotic suggestions 
depends on the  
individual's inner desires and willingness to accept or reject the suggestion.  
Waking Suggestion 
Waking suggestions are made during normal waking consciousness, without formal 
hypnosis or  
trance induction. These suggestions are effective even without a hypnotic state, as 
they can still  
bypass the critical, analytical mind. The response to waking suggestions is not limited 
to a trance  
state, and individuals can be influenced by them throughout daily life. For example, a 
child feels  
better after a mother kisses a scraped knee because the child believes and accepts the 
suggestion  
that it will heal. Waking suggestions also occur in advertising, media, and social 
interactions, where  
external cues influence thoughts and behavior. 
Waking Trance 
A waking trance occurs when individuals are in a relaxed, receptive state due to 
environmental  
factors or external influences, such as being in a hospital or hearing certain comments. 
In these  
situations, people are more likely to accept suggestions because their critical faculties 
are  
bypassed. For example, a person may feel hotter after hearing someone mention how 
hot the  
weather is. Waking trance often involves the acceptance of suggestions either by 
agreeing with  
them or by not actively rejecting them. 
Rejecting Suggestions  
  
Everyone has an inner sense of knowing, which helps them filter suggestions. If a 
suggestion is  
perceived as harmful, or goes against deeply held beliefs or values, it will be rejected. 
The subject  
may ignore the suggestion, shut down any further suggestions, or mentally alter the 
suggestion to

fit their own perception. This highlights the importance of the subject’s personal 
awareness and  
ability to selectively choose which suggestions to accept.

label: Chapter 3
title: Suggestibility
content:
Chapter - 4 Suggestibility 
 
Suggestibility is a 
fundamental concept in hypnosis, reflecting the degree to 
which an individual is responsive to suggestions. Understanding the relationship 
between 
suggestibility and hypnosis can shed light on how hypnotherapy and hypnotic 
techniques 
work. Suggestibility is very important in hypnosis because it affects how well a 
person can 
enter a hypnotic state and respond to suggestions. When someone is more 
suggestible, they 
can more easily relax and focus during hypnosis, making it easier for the 
hypnotist to guide 
them. This makes the hypnosis session more effective, especially for therapeutic 
purposes 
like reducing anxiety, quitting smoking, or changing other behaviours. People 
who are 
highly suggestible tend to trust the hypnotist and the process more, which helps 
them to 
follow suggestions and achieve better results. Understanding a person's level of 
suggestibility also helps the hypnotist to tailor their approach, ensuring that the 
session is as 
effective as possible. 
 
 
Types of Suggestibility 
 
Based on the context in which suggestions are made and the individual's 
responsiveness, 
suggestibility can be categorized, each playing a unique role in how individuals 
accept and 
act upon suggestions. In hypnosis, physical suggestibility and emotional 
suggestibility are two different types of responsiveness to hypnotic suggestions

based on their predominant 
response styles—either physical or emotional. These concepts help hypnotists 
modify their 
approach to the individual’s natural tendencies. 
 
 
Physical Suggestibility 
 
Physical suggestibility refers to a person's tendency to respond to suggestions 
that 
involve physical sensations and motor activities. Individuals with high physical 
suggestibility are more likely to respond to direct, literal, and sensory-based 
suggestions. They often show noticeable physical reactions to hypnotic 
suggestions. 
 
Characteristics and Examples: 
 
- Direct and Literal Suggestions: Physically suggestible individuals respond well 
to straightforward suggestions like "Your arm is getting lighter" or "You feel a 
warm 
sensation in your hand." 
 
- Sensory Focus: 
These individuals are more affected by suggestions involving 
physical sensations, such as feeling warmth, heaviness, or tingling. 
 
- Observable Reactions:

They may exhibit visible physical responses, such as 
moving an arm, closing eyes, or relaxing muscles. 
 
Example: 
If a hypnotist suggests that the person’s hand is stuck to a table, a 
physically suggestible person might genuinely feel as if their hand cannot move 
and 
show physical signs of struggling to lift it. 
 
 
Emotional Suggestibility 
 
Emotional suggestibility refers to a person's tendency to respond to suggestions 
that 
involve emotions, feelings, and abstract concepts. Individuals with high 
emotional 
suggestibility are more likely to respond to indirect, metaphorical, and 
emotionally 
charged suggestions. They often exhibit subtle, internalized reactions to hypnotic 
suggestions. 
 
 
Characteristics and Examples: 
 
- Indirect and Metaphorical Suggestions: 
Emotionally suggestible individuals 
respond better to suggestions that are abstract or emotional, such as "Imagine a 
wave 
of calmness washing over you" or "You feel a deep sense of peace."

- Emotional Focus: These individuals are more affected by suggestions involving 
emotions and feelings, such as feeling more confident, happy, or relaxed. 
 
Delicate Reactions: Their responses might be more internal and less visible, such 
as a change in mood or emotional state. 
 
Example: If a hypnotist suggests that the person feels a growing sense of inner 
peace, an emotionally suggestible person might experience a profound sense of 
calm 
and emotional relief, even though there may not be any noticeable physical 
changes. 
 
 
Somnambulism 
 
A somnambulist, in the context of hypnosis, refers to a person who exhibits a 
high 
degree of hypnotic suggestibility and responsiveness. This type will respond 
equally 
well to all suggestions, both direct and inferred, affecting both the body and the 
emotions. These individuals are often able to enter deep states of hypnosis 
quickly 
and readily accept and act upon suggestions from the hypnotist. 
 
Characteristics and Examples: 
 
-High Suggestibility: Somnambulists are highly responsive to hypnotic 
suggestions. They can easily enter deep hypnotic states.

-Absorption: They have a heightened ability to concentrate and focus inwardly 
during hypnosis. This absorption allows them to vividly imagine scenarios 
suggested 
by the hypnotist and experience them as real. 
 
-Amnesia: Somnambulists may experience selective amnesia during hypnosis, 
where they forget certain suggestions or experiences after emerging from the 
hypnotic state. 
 
-Physical Responses: They may exhibit physical responses to suggestions, such as 
changes in muscle tone, temperature perception, or other physical sensations 
suggested by the hypnotist. 
 
Example: 
Imagine a hypnotherapist working with a client who wants to overcome a fear of 
public speaking. During the induction phase the hypnotherapist suggests that the 
client feels confident, calm, and in control whenever they imagine themselves 
speaking in front of others. The somnambulist not only visualizes this scenario 
vividly but also experiences a sense of relaxation and confidence deep within. 
 
Intellectual suggestibility 
 
Intellectual suggestibility refers to a person's tendency to respond to suggestions 
based on cognitive reasoning and intellectual understanding. Individuals high in 
intellectual suggestibility often analyse suggestions critically and may require 
logical explanations or justifications before accepting them. 
 
Characteristics and Examples:

Analytical Thinking: Individuals with intellectual suggestibility tend to evaluate 
the logic, coherence, and reasoning behind suggestions before accepting them. 
 
-Need for Explanation: They often require clear explanations or justifications for 
the suggestions provided during hypnosis. 
 
-Preference for Rationality: Intellectual suggestible rely on rationality and logic 
to 
assess the validity and effectiveness of suggestions. They may engage in a process 
of reasoning and evaluation before deciding to adopt a suggestion. 
 
-Open to Logical Arguments: They are more receptive to suggestions that are 
backed by evidence or reasoned explanations. 
 
Example: 
Imagine a hypnotherapist working with a client who wants to overcome a fear of 
flying. The client, who exhibits high intellectual suggestibility, engages in a 
detailed 
discussion with the hypnotherapist about the safety measures in place on modern 
aircraft, statistical safety records, and the principles of aerodynamics. During the 
hypnosis session, the hypnotherapist carefully explains how the subconscious 
mind 
can be reassured through positive suggestions about flying safety, backed by 
logical 
reasoning and evidence-based information. 
 
How Suggestibility is learned? 
Suggestibility is influenced by a combination of innate tendencies and learned 
experiences. 
While some aspects of suggestibility might be inherent, many factors that shape 
suggestibility are acquired through personal experiences, social interactions, and 
environmental influences. Here's a detailed look at how suggestibility is learned.

Innate Tendencies 
 
1. Temperament: Some people are naturally more open to suggestions due to 
their 
temperament. Those who are more agreeable or empathetic may inherently be 
more 
suggestible. 
2. Genetics: There may be a genetic component that influences individuals to 
higher 
or lower levels of suggestibility, although this is still an area of ongoing research. 
 
Learned Experiences 
 
1. Early Childhood: 
Parental Influence: Children learn suggestibility from their parents and 
caregivers. 
A child who is often reassured and whose feelings are validated may grow up to 
be 
more emotionally suggestible. 
Environment: A stable, supportive environment can lead to higher emotional 
suggestibility, while a more challenging or unpredictable environment might 
foster 
lower suggestibility or a more physically based response to suggestions. 
 
2. Education and Socialization: 
School and Peer Interactions: Teachers, peers, and school experiences play a 
significant role in shaping suggestibility. Positive reinforcement from authority 
figures can enhance suggestibility. 
Social Dynamics: Individuals learn to be more or less suggestible based on their 
social interactions. Peer pressure and social conformity can increase 
suggestibility

3. Cultural Factors: 
Cultural Norms: Cultures that value collectivism and conformity may foster 
higher 
suggestibility, while cultures that value individualism and scepticism may foster 
lower suggestibility. 
Media Influence: Exposure to media and societal norms can shape suggestibility 
by 
consistently presenting certain ideas and behaviours as desirable or acceptable. 
 
 
4. Personal Experiences: 
Traumatic Experiences: Trauma can alter suggestibility. For example, 
individuals 
who have experienced significant trauma may become more suggestible to 
certain 
triggers or protective suggestions. 
Positive Reinforcement: Experiences where suggestions led to positive outcomes 
can reinforce suggestibility. For example, if a suggestion to try a new activity 
leads 
to enjoyment and success, the person may become more open to future 
suggestions. 
 
 
Psychological and Cognitive Factors 
 
1. Self-Esteem: People with lower self-esteem might be more suggestible as they 
may 
rely more on external validation and guidance. 
2. Cognitive Development: As people grow and their cognitive abilities develop, 
their 
level of critical thinking and distrust can influence their suggestibility. Children 
and 
adolescents are generally more suggestible than adults. 
3. Hypnotisability: Some individuals have a higher natural capacity for hypnosis,

which can indicate higher general suggestibility. This trait can be enhanced or 
diminished through practice and experience. 
 
Examples of How Suggestibility is Learned 
1. In the Family: 
Scenario: A child grows up in a household where parents frequently use positive 
reinforcement. 
Outcome: The child learns to trust and follow suggestions from authority figures, 
developing higher emotional suggestibility. 
2. In Education: 
Scenario: A student is praised by a teacher for following instructions carefully 
and 
performing well on assignments. 
Outcome: The student learns that accepting and acting on suggestions leads to 
positive outcomes, reinforcing suggestibility. 
3. In Social Settings: 
Scenario: A teenager joins a peer group where conformity is valued and 
rewarded. 
Outcome: The teenager learns to be more suggestible to peer influences to gain 
acceptance and approval. 
 
4. Through Media: 
Scenario: An individual watches a lot of advertisements that use emotional 
appeals. 
Outcome: The individual learns to associate certain products with positive 
emotions, increasing suggestibility to advertising. 
 
Strategies for Understanding and Working with 
 
1) Modifying Communication: By understanding someone's background and 
experiences, you can adjust your suggestions to better suit how they think and 
respond. This makes it easier for them to accept and follow your suggestions. 
2) Building Trust: When you build a trusting relationship with someone, they

feel safe 
and are more likely to be open to your suggestions. Trust makes them more 
comfortable and willing to listen to you. 
 
3) Providing Positive Reinforcement: When you praise or reward someone for 
responding well to a suggestion, it encourages them to continue accepting 
suggestions in the future. Positive feedback makes them feel good about their 
progress. 
 
4) Encouraging Critical Thinking: Teaching people to think carefully and 
critically 
helps them evaluate suggestions better. This way, they can choose to accept only 
those suggestions that are beneficial and make sense to them, leading to better 
outcomes. 
By recognizing the factors that contribute to learned suggestibility, one can 
better 
understand and effectively interact with individuals according to their 
suggestibility 
type.

label: Chapter 4
title: Suggestibility Questionnaire
content:
Suggestibility Questionnaire  
 
A suggestibility questionnaire is designed specifically to assess physical or emotional 
suggestibility and it focus on how individuals respond to suggestions through either physical 
sensations or emotional experiences. This questionnaire is to understand how you take in or 
interpret information. As this trait is frozen during teen-age and remains constant thereafter, 
most of the questions need you to think of situations during your teenage and how you 
responded to them most of the time.  
The following is a conditioned response test designed to help Hypnotherapist assist you 
more rapidly with your program. It is important that you answer these questions truthfully, 
keeping in mind there are no “right” or “wrong” answers. Go with the first answer that 
comes to mind after you read the question. Try not to dwell on any question. Simply check 
“Yes” or “No” beside each question. If there has been no practical experience, imagine how 
you might have interacted during your teenage in those situations and answer. Please answer 
all the questions in both sheets. Follow instructions on score sheet below.   
Scoring 
1. Count the number of YES answers to Questionnaire 1. Question no. 1 and 2 score 
10 points each if answered YES. Each remaining (question 3-18) YES scores 5 
points each.  
2. Total each Questionnaire separately for both the applicable '10' and '5' YES scores.  
3. Add the totals of both Questionnaires to find the COMBINED Total.  
4. Divide the score of Questionnaire 1 by the Combined Total and multiply it with 100. 
This indicates the percentage of Physical suggestibility. 
5. Subtract the score from 100 to determine the percentage of Emotional suggestibility. 
 
Physical Suggestibility:  
A/(A+B)  X 100 = _______ %  (PS) 
Emotional Suggestibility: 
100 – (PS)  =  _______ % (ES) 
Indications in Suggestibility Questionnaire 
 
“YES” answers to Questionnaire No. 1 are indicative of Physical Suggestibility as 
they show physical suggestible behaviour. 
 
“YES” answers to Questionnaire No. 2 are indicative of Emotional Suggestibility as 
they show emotional suggestible behaviour.
